entities.name: STRIGA ASIATICA
entities.description: A parasitic weed that affects sorghum roots

entities.name: SORGHUM
entities.description: The host crop used in the study

entities.name: SOIL TEMPERATURE
entities.description: The temperature of the soil which affects Striga seed viability and germination

entities.name: SOIL MOISTURE
entities.description: The water content of the soil affecting Striga seed germination and emergence

entities.name: NITROGEN
entities.description: A nutrient whose application rate affects Striga emergence and sorghum vigor

entities.name: SEED GERMINATION
entities.description: The process by which Striga seeds sprout

entities.name: VIABILITY
entities.description: The ability of Striga seeds to remain alive and capable of germinating

entities.name: EMERGENCE
entities.description: The appearance of Striga plants above the soil surface

entities.name: SOLARIZATION
entities.description: The use of clear polythene to increase soil temperature to kill seeds

entities.name: HAY MULCH
entities.description: A treatment used to vary soil temperature

entities.name: BARE SOIL
entities.description: A treatment used to vary soil temperature

entities.name: IRRIGATION
entities.description: The application of water to change soil moisture levels

entities.name: NITROGEN APPLICATION
entities.description: The process of applying nitrogen at different rates to the soil

entities.name: VIGOR
entities.description: The overall health and growth strength of the sorghum plants

entities.name: STRIGA INCIDENCE
entities.description: The occurrence and prevalence of Striga parasitism

entities.name: ROOT EXUDATE
entities.description: Chemicals released by sorghum roots that trigger Striga seed germination

relationships.description: High soil temperature from solarization prevents germination
High soil temperature from solarization reduces seed viability
Irrigation levels did not have significant effects on germination
Irrigation levels did not have significant effects on viability
Higher irrigation levels slightly increased the number of emerged plants
Striga emergence is directly related to the rate of nitrogen application
Nitrogen application neutralizes the harmful effects of Striga on sorghum vigor
Sorghum root exudates are required for Striga seed germination
Solarization increases the maximum soil temperature
Irrigation is used to change soil moisture levels
Increasing nitrogen application rates increases the number of emerged Striga plants
Nitrogen application does not reduce Striga incidence
